topic: Two siblings inherit their grandmother's house
raw_output:
[story 1]

The solicitor’s crisp voice echoed in the cavernous entryway, detailing the division of Grandmother Elara’s estate. I stood by the dusty mahogany table, my jaw tight enough to crack enamel. My brother, Mark, was already practically vibrating with excitement, a wide, careless grin splitting his face as he surveyed the antique furniture. I felt a slow, cold tightening in my chest, a sensation like a stone settling deep in my ribs. "Well," I managed, my voice sounding unnaturally flat, "this is... a lot of responsibility." Mark merely waved a dismissive hand, his gaze already drifting towards the sprawling garden. "Don't be such a stick in the mud, Alex. We'll figure it out." The casualness of his tone struck me like a physical blow, making my knuckles whiten where I gripped the edge of the table. I took a deliberate step back, creating distance between us, the air suddenly feeling too thin to breathe.

[story 2]

I traced the faded floral wallpaper with a fingertip, the silence of the old house amplifying the sound of my own shallow breathing. When Mark finally spoke, his tone dripping with condescension, I flinched almost imperceptibly. "So, you’re going to sit around cataloging dusty relics, are you?" he asked, leaning against a towering bookcase as if I were an inconvenient piece of furniture. I finally looked up, my eyes locking onto his, and I could feel a distinct heat rising in my neck. "I intend to handle things properly," I replied, my voice low and steady, though the tremor in my hand betraying the composure I was desperately trying to maintain. He scoffed, a short, sharp sound devoid of warmth. "Properly? You wouldn't know how to manage anything beyond organizing old photographs, Alex. I’ll handle the actual logistics." He pushed off the shelf, his posture radiating an undeniable sense of superiority that made my stomach clench.

[story 3]

The scent of aged lavender and decaying wood hung heavy in the air. I watched Mark meticulously unpack a box of porcelain dolls, humming a tuneless melody that grated on my nerves. When he turned to me, expecting some sort of shared enthusiasm, I simply crossed my arms tightly over my chest. The movement was stiff, deliberate. "I was thinking we could start with the downstairs," I announced, my tone clipped and utterly devoid of inflection. Mark stopped sorting the items, his brow furrowing in theatrical surprise. "Oh, look who decided to show up," he drawled, his eyes flicking over my posture as if judging my entire presence. "I’ll take the main rooms. You can manage the storage attic, I suppose. It’s hardly a task requiring finesse." The way he said 'storage attic'—as if it were beneath my capabilities—sent a distinct wave of irritation washing over me, making my shoulders tense up beneath my shirt. I just stared at him, refusing to meet his gaze directly.
